docs.title: 🚀 DexaMinds API Guidelines
docs.text:
Welcome to the DexaMinds API Guidelines! This collection of documents outlines the standards, best practices, and conventions for designing, developing, and maintaining APIs across our platform.

Following these guidelines ensures consistency, improves developer experience, and enhances security and performance across all services.

Please refer to the respective sections for REST, GraphQL, versioning, error handling, and more.

docs.title: 📋 Quick Navigation
docs.text:
• REST API Guidelines - Standards for RESTful API design
  • GraphQL Guidelines - Best practices for GraphQL APIs
  • Error Handling - Standardized error responses and codes
  • Versioning - API versioning strategy
  • Security - Security best practices
  • Design Principles - Core API design philosophy

docs.title: 🎯 Purpose
docs.text:
These guidelines ensure consistency, improve developer experience, and enhance security and performance across all DexaMinds services. Following these standards helps maintain a cohesive API ecosystem that is:

  • Intuitive: Easy to understand and use
  • Consistent: Follows predictable patterns
  • Maintainable: Easy to update and extend
  • Secure: Built with security in mind

docs.title: 🛠 Style & Formatting
docs.text:
• Use clear, concise language
  • Follow the Google API Design Guide principles
  • Maintain consistent formatting across all API documentation
  • Include practical examples for all endpoints and types

docs.title: 🔄 Maintenance
docs.text:
1. Keep it current
  2. Update guidelines when introducing new patterns or technologies

  3. Review and revise guidelines quarterly

  4. Versioning

  5. Document breaking changes clearly

  6. Maintain backward compatibility when possible

  7. Documentation

  8. Include code samples in multiple languages
  9. Document all parameters and responses
  10. Provide context and examples for complex operations

docs.title: 🤝 Contributing
docs.text:
We welcome contributions to improve these guidelines! Please follow these steps:

  1. Open an issue to discuss proposed changes
  2. Create a pull request with your updates
  3. Request review from the API Guild

docs.title: ℹ️ Additional Resources
docs.text:
• OpenAPI Specification
  • GraphQL Best Practices
  • REST API Tutorial

Last updated: June 2024

docs.title: API Design Principles
docs.text: Dexaminds API design is grounded in clarity, consistency, and usability. The following principles guide our decisions:

docs.title: 1. Resource-Centric
docs.text:
APIs should expose resources using RESTful URIs:

GET /projects/42/tasks/5

docs.title: 2. Consistency
docs.text: Follow naming conventions, response formats, and status codes to provide a uniform developer experience.

docs.title: 3. Simplicity
docs.text: Design APIs to be intuitive and easy to consume with minimal learning curve.

docs.title: 4. Evolvability
docs.text: Structure APIs to allow future improvements without breaking existing consumers.

docs.title: 5. Documentation
docs.text: Every API must be accompanied by accurate, versioned, and machine-readable documentation (e.g., OpenAPI specs).

docs.title: Error Handling Guidelines
docs.text: Standardized error handling ensures developers can quickly identify and resolve issues.

docs.title: Response Format
docs.text:
Every error should follow this structure:

{
  "error": "Invalid input data",
  "code": "INVALID_INPUT",
  "status": 400
}

docs.title: Status Codes
docs.text:
• 400 Bad Request
  • 401 Unauthorized
  • 403 Forbidden
  • 404 Not Found
  • 409 Conflict
  • 422 Validation Error
  • 500 Internal Server Error

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Never expose stack traces
  • Include error codes and user-friendly messages
  • Return validation details when available:
{
  "error": "Validation failed",
  "fields": {
    "email": "Invalid email address"
  }
}

docs.title: GraphQL API Guidelines
docs.text: When using GraphQL, the following conventions must be followed to ensure maintainability and efficiency.

docs.title: Schema Design
docs.text:
• Modularize the schema by feature/domain
  • Avoid exposing internal logic or data models

docs.title: Naming
docs.text:
Use meaningful, descriptive names:

query GetActiveUsers { ... }
mutation UpdateProfile { ... }

docs.title: Pagination
docs.text: Use the Relay-style edges and pageInfo approach to paginate large datasets.

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
• Require authentication on all queries/mutations
  • Validate input fields rigorously

docs.title: Versioning
docs.text: Prefer to evolve the schema rather than introduce breaking changes. Use deprecation flags in the schema.

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: Provide clear error messages through the errors field. Avoid stack traces or internal messages in production.

docs.title: REST API Guidelines
docs.text: These guidelines apply to all RESTful APIs developed within Dexaminds.

docs.title: URL Conventions
docs.text:
• Use plural nouns: /users, /tasks
  • Nested resources: /projects/123/tasks
  • Avoid verbs in paths: ❌ /getUser

docs.title: HTTP Methods
docs.text:
• GET: Retrieve
  • POST: Create
  • PUT: Replace
  • PATCH: Update
  • DELETE: Remove

docs.title: Status Codes
docs.text:
• 200 OK
  • 201 Created
  • 204 No Content
  • 400 Bad Request
  • 404 Not Found
  • 500 Internal Server Error

docs.title: Query Parameters
docs.text:
Use for filters, pagination, and sorting:

GET /users?page=2&role=admin

docs.title: Response Format
docs.text: Use JSON with camelCase field names. Always return consistent structure.

docs.title: Pagination
docs.text:
Use:

{
  "data": [...],
  "meta": { "page": 1, "limit": 10, "total": 120 }
}

docs.title: API Security Best Practices
docs.text: Security is a non-negotiable pillar of our API development process.

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
• Use OAuth 2.0 or JWT
  • Tokens must be sent via Authorization headers

docs.title: Authorization
docs.text:
• Enforce access control based on user roles/permissions
  • Never rely on frontend validation alone

docs.title: Input Validation
docs.text:
• Sanitize all input data
  • Use schema validation to prevent injection attacks

docs.title: HTTPS Enforcement
docs.text:
• All APIs must be served over TLS (HTTPS)
  • Reject plain HTTP requests at the edge

docs.title: Rate Limiting
docs.text:
• Apply per-user and per-IP rate limits
  • Use HTTP 429 for rate limit violations

docs.title: Logging & Auditing
docs.text:
• Log all authentication attempts and data access events
  • Monitor and alert on suspicious activity

docs.title: Secret Management
docs.text:
• Use GitHub Secrets or secure vaults for environment credentials
  • Never commit secrets in code or logs

docs.title: API Versioning
docs.text: API versioning is essential to safely introduce breaking changes and support long-term clients.

docs.title: When to Version
docs.text:
• Removing or renaming endpoints
  • Changing request/response formats
  • Modifying authentication or error handling

docs.title: Recommended Strategy
docs.text:
Use URI-based versioning:

GET /v1/users

docs.title: Deprecation Policy
docs.text:
• Announce deprecated endpoints clearly in documentation
  • Provide support for legacy versions for a minimum of 6 months

docs.title: Versioning Rules
docs.text:
• Use semantic versioning when possible (v1, v2, etc.)
  • Avoid query param or header-based versioning in public APIs

docs.title: 🏗️ DexaMinds Engineering Handbook
docs.text: Welcome to the DexaMinds Engineering Handbook! This repository serves as the central hub for our engineering standards, best practices, and team guidelines. It's designed to help our team maintain consistency, quality, and efficiency across all our engineering efforts.

docs.title: 📚 Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Coding Standards - Language-specific coding guidelines and best practices
  2. Development Practices - Our approach to software development and delivery
  3. Team Culture - Our engineering values and collaboration guidelines
  4. Career Growth - Resources and frameworks for professional development

docs.title: 🎯 Purpose
docs.text:
This handbook is a living document that evolves with our team and technology stack. It's intended to:

  • Provide clear guidelines for maintaining code quality
  • Document our engineering processes and best practices
  • Help onboard new team members efficiently
  • Serve as a reference for technical decision-making

docs.title: 🛠 How to Use This Handbook
docs.text:
1. For New Team Members: Start with the Onboarding Guide
  2. Contributing: See CONTRIBUTING.md for how to suggest changes

docs.title: 🔄 Keeping This Handbook Updated
docs.text: We encourage all team members to contribute to this handbook. If you find something that's outdated or missing, please submit a pull request or create an issue.

docs.title: Leveling Framework
docs.text: At DexaMinds, we recognize that growth isn’t linear — it’s multidimensional. Our engineering leveling framework provides a clear, flexible path for personal development, technical mastery, and leadership. This framework is not just about promotions; it's a tool for self-reflection, career conversations, and intentional growth.

docs.title: 🎯 Purpose
docs.text:
• Define expectations at each level of engineering contribution.
  • Foster transparency, consistency, and fairness in evaluations.
  • Encourage ownership of one’s growth journey through structured guidance.

docs.title: 🧭 Career Tracks
docs.text:
We offer two equally respected tracks:

  • Individual Contributor (IC) — Deep technical expertise, ownership of systems, thought leadership.
  • Engineering Manager (EM) — People leadership, team orchestration, delivery accountability.

Transitions between tracks are supported and celebrated.

docs.title: 📶 IC Levels
docs.text: Level Title Focus L1 Software Engineer I Learning, onboarding, executing well-defined tasks L2 Software Engineer II Independent contributor, reliable execution L3 Senior Engineer End-to-end ownership, mentorship, architectural input L4 Staff Engineer Technical strategy, system design, cross-team impact L5 Principal Engineer Org-wide influence, visionary innovation, thought leadership

docs.title: 🧱 Core Competencies (IC Track)
docs.text:
• Technical Mastery: Proficiency in core technologies and system design.
  • Execution: Ability to deliver features with quality, speed, and care.
  • Collaboration: Empathetic communication and cross-functional partnership.
  • Impact: Measurable contributions to product, platform, or team velocity.
  • Growth Mindset: Seeks feedback, self-improves, and supports others' growth.

docs.title: 🧑‍🤝‍🧑 EM Levels
docs.text: Level Title Focus L3M Engineering Manager Team management, delivery leadership L4M Senior Engineering Manager Multi-team leadership, cross-functional strategy L5M Director of Engineering Org leadership, vision alignment, scaling teams

docs.title: 📂 Core Competencies (EM Track)
docs.text:
• People Development: Coaching, mentoring, performance support.
  • Team Execution: Roadmap planning, delivery velocity, risk mitigation.
  • Culture Stewardship: Psychological safety, inclusion, team morale.
  • Technical Guidance: Systems thinking, architectural review, code quality.
  • Business Alignment: Translating product strategy into engineering outcomes.

docs.title: 📊 Growth Evaluations
docs.text:
• Self-Assessment: Conducted every 6 months using the leveling rubric.
  • Manager Feedback: Regular 1:1s and performance reviews.
  • 360 Feedback: Peer input and cross-functional insights.
  • Promotion Panels: Transparent, committee-based decisions using evidence and artifacts.

docs.title: 🔁 Continuous Growth Model
docs.text:
• Growth is ongoing — not reserved for review cycles.
  • Promotion is recognition of consistent, sustained performance.
  • Movement within or across tracks is encouraged and supported.

docs.title: 🪴 Our Philosophy
docs.text:
At DexaMinds, your career isn’t a ladder — it’s a garden. You choose what to grow, how to grow, and who to grow with.

We’re here to provide the soil, water, and sunlight. You bring the curiosity, courage, and craft.

docs.title: Mentorship
docs.text:
Mentorship is a cornerstone of the DexaMinds engineering culture. We believe that greatness is not achieved in isolation — it is cultivated through shared wisdom, trusted guidance, and meaningful human connections.

Our mentorship program is designed to empower every engineer to thrive — whether you’re just starting out or preparing to lead large systems or teams.

docs.title: 🎯 Objectives
docs.text:
• Accelerate growth through guidance, not gatekeeping.
  • Build trusted relationships that foster psychological safety.
  • Promote a culture of giving back and lifting others as we rise.
  • Support both technical and non-technical aspects of career growth.

docs.title: 👥 Mentorship Models
docs.text: We support different types of mentorship based on context and goals:

docs.title: 1. Onboarding Mentorship
docs.text:
• Assigned to every new engineer.
  • Helps navigate tools, systems, and culture during the first 90 days.

docs.title: 2. Technical Mentorship
docs.text:
• Deep dive into specific domains (e.g., DevOps, Frontend, ML).
  • Often led by senior engineers or domain experts.

docs.title: 3. Career Mentorship
docs.text:
• Focused on long-term growth, goal setting, and leveling up.
  • Conversations often include feedback, confidence building, and leadership.

docs.title: 4. Peer Mentorship
docs.text:
• Informal, lateral support from colleagues.
  • Encourages vulnerability, problem-solving, and shared learning.

docs.title: 🔄 How It Works
docs.text:
• Kickoff: New engineers are paired with a mentor within the first week.
  • Cadence: Regular check-ins (weekly or bi-weekly) encouraged.
  • Tooling: Notion templates and prompts to guide conversations.
  • Tracking: Outcomes and growth plans are logged collaboratively.

docs.title: 💡 Tips for Mentees
docs.text:
• Be proactive — mentorship is a two-way street.
  • Ask questions, share goals, and own your journey.
  • Respect your mentor’s time and come prepared.

docs.title: 🌱 Tips for Mentors
docs.text:
• Listen more than you speak.
  • Share both successes and failures.
  • Adapt your style to the mentee’s needs.
  • Celebrate growth — big or small.

docs.title: ✨ Culture of Mentorship
docs.text:
At DexaMinds, mentorship is not a title or a program — it's a mindset. We believe:

  • Everyone has something to teach, and everyone has something to learn.
  • Mentorship should be reciprocal, empowering, and joyful.
  • The impact of a good mentor extends far beyond technical skill — it shapes careers, confidence, and culture.

"True leadership is planting seeds in a garden you may never see bloom."

Let's continue planting boldly.

docs.title: 📝 Coding Standards
docs.text: This directory contains language-specific coding standards and guidelines for the DexaMinds engineering team. Consistent coding standards help maintain code quality, readability, and collaboration across our codebase.

docs.title: 📚 Available Standards
docs.text:
1. JavaScript Guidelines for JavaScript/TypeScript development, including formatting, naming conventions, and best practices.

  2. Python Python coding standards covering style, documentation, and common patterns.

docs.title: 🎯 General Principles
docs.text:
1. Readability First
  2. Code is read more often than written
  3. Favor clarity over cleverness

  4. Use meaningful names

  5. Consistency

  6. Follow the style of existing code
  7. Be consistent within a file

  8. Be consistent with the language's idioms

  9. Documentation

  10. Document public APIs
  11. Include examples for complex logic
  12. Keep comments up-to-date

docs.title: JavaScript/TypeScript
docs.text:
• Linter: ESLint
  • Formatter: Prettier
  • Type Checking: TypeScript

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
• Linter: flake8
  • Formatter: Black
  • Import Sorter: isort

docs.title: 🔄 Code Review Guidelines
docs.text: When reviewing code, check for: - Adherence to these standards - Consistent style with the codebase - Clear and helpful comments - Proper error handling - Test coverage

docs.title: 📝 Adding New Standards
docs.text: To add or update coding standards: 1. Update the appropriate language file 2. Include examples of both good and bad patterns 3. Get approval from the engineering team 4. Update this README if needed

docs.title: ❓ Need Help?
docs.text:
• For JavaScript/TypeScript questions: #frontend-help
  • For Python questions: #backend-help
  • For general coding standards: #engineering-standards

Last Updated: June 2025

docs.title: 1. General Guidelines
docs.text:
• Follow the Airbnb Style Guide
  • Use const and let (avoid var)
  • Prefer modern ES6+ features

docs.title: 2. File and Variable Naming
docs.text:
• Use camelCase for variables/functions
  • Use PascalCase for components/classes
  • File names: kebab-case.js (e.g., user-profile.js)

docs.title: 3. Formatting
docs.text:
• 2-space indentation
  • Always use semicolons
  • Prefer single quotes
  • Use trailing commas in multi-line objects/arrays
const user = {
  name: 'Alice',
  age: 30,
};

docs.title: 4. Functions
docs.text:
• Use arrow functions for anonymous and short functions
const greet = name => `Hello, ${name}`;
  • Avoid deeply nested callbacks (prefer async/await or Promises)

docs.title: 5. Comments & Documentation
docs.text:
• Use // for single-line, /** */ for multi-line
  • Use JSDoc for public functions
/**
 * Calculate sum of two numbers.
 * @param {number} a - First number
 * @param {number} b - Second number
 * @returns {number}
 */
function add(a, b) {
  return a + b;
}

docs.title: 6. Objects & Arrays
docs.text:
• Use destructuring where appropriate
const { name, age } = user;
  • Avoid mutation — prefer immutable updates

docs.title: 7. Error Handling
docs.text: • Store constants and helper functions in their own files

docs.title: 8. State Management
docs.text:
• Use state management libraries (Redux, Context API) for global state
  • Keep state as local as possible, lift state up when needed

docs.title: 9. Performance Optimization
docs.text:
• Use useMemo and useCallback for expensive calculations and callbacks
  • Implement code splitting for large applications
  • Use React.memo for preventing unnecessary re-renders

docs.title: 10. Security Practices
docs.text:
• Sanitize all user inputs
  • Use environment variables for sensitive data
  • Implement proper CORS policies
  • Use Content Security Policy (CSP) headers

docs.title: 11. Testing
docs.text:
• Write unit tests with Jest
  • Aim for 80%+ test coverage
  • Test user interactions with React Testing Library

docs.title: 12. Error Boundaries
docs.text:
• Implement error boundaries to catch JavaScript errors
  • Provide fallback UIs for better user experience

docs.title: 13. Accessibility (a11y)
docs.text:
• Use semantic HTML elements
  • Add proper ARIA attributes
  • Ensure keyboard navigation works
  • Test with screen readers

docs.title: 14. Code Organization
docs.text:
• Follow the "Feature First" folder structure
  • Keep related files together (component + styles + tests)
  • Separate business logic from UI components

docs.title: 15. Performance Monitoring
docs.text:
• Implement error tracking (Sentry, LogRocket)
  • Monitor Core Web Vitals
  • Use React DevTools Profiler

docs.title: 16. Internationalization (i18n)
docs.text:
• Externalize all UI strings
  • Use libraries like react-i18next
  • Consider RTL (Right-to-Left) layouts

docs.title: 17. Code Reviews
docs.text:
• Keep PRs small and focused
  • Include screenshots for UI changes
  • Address all review comments before merging
  • Use meaningful commit messages

docs.title: 1. General Guidelines
docs.text:
• Follow PEP 8
  • Max line length: 79 characters
  • Prefer clarity over cleverness
  • Avoid magic numbers, use named constants

docs.title: 2. File Naming
docs.text:
• Use snake_case for Python file names, e.g., data_loader.py
  • Avoid dashes or capital letters in filenames

docs.title: 3. Indentation and Spacing
docs.text:
• Use 4 spaces for indentation (no tabs)
  • Add blank lines between top-level functions and classes

docs.title: 4. Imports
docs.text:
• Standard library imports first, then third-party, then project-specific
  • Use absolute imports when possible
# Good
import os
import pandas as pd
from myproject.utils import helper

docs.title: 5. Docstrings
docs.text:
• Use triple double-quoted strings """ per PEP 257
def fetch_data(source: str) -> dict:
    """
    Fetch data from a specified source.

    Args:
        source (str): The data source path.

    Returns:
        dict: Parsed data from the source.
    """
    pass

docs.title: 6. Naming Conventions
docs.text:
• snake_case for variables/functions
  • UPPER_CASE for constants
  • PascalCase for class names

docs.title: 7. Functions
docs.text:
• Keep them short and focused
  • Prefer keyword arguments for clarity
  • Include type hints

docs.title: 8. Classes
docs.text:
• Single responsibility per class
  • Group related methods
  • Use __init__ constructor wisely

docs.title: 9. Error Handling
docs.text:
• Use try...except blocks for error handling
  • Dependency management: pip-tools or poetry

docs.title: 10. Type Hinting
docs.text:
• Use type hints for all function parameters and return values
  • Leverage typing module for complex types
  • Add # type: ignore only when absolutely necessary

docs.title: 11. Virtual Environments
docs.text:
• Always use virtual environments for project dependencies
  • Document all dependencies in requirements.txt or pyproject.toml
  • Pin dependency versions for production

docs.title: 12. Logging
docs.text:
• Use the logging module instead of print statements
  • Configure appropriate log levels (DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR)
  • Include contextual information in log messages

docs.title: 13. Testing
docs.text:
• Write unit tests using pytest
  • Follow the Arrange-Act-Assert pattern
  • Aim for 80%+ test coverage
  • Use fixtures for test dependencies

docs.title: 14. Performance
docs.text:
• Use list/dict comprehensions for simple loops
  • Leverage generators for large datasets
  • Profile code before optimizing (cProfile, line_profiler)

docs.title: 15. Security
docs.text:
• Use secrets module for cryptographic operations
  • Sanitize all user inputs
  • Avoid eval() and exec() with user input
  • Use parameterized queries for database operations

docs.title: 16. Documentation
docs.text:
• Keep docstrings up-to-date with code changes
  • Use Sphinx or MkDocs for project documentation
  • Include examples in docstrings

docs.title: 17. Code Quality
docs.text:
• Run linters (pylint, flake8) in CI/CD
  • Use black for code formatting
  • Run mypy for static type checking

docs.title: 18. Concurrency
docs.text:
• Use asyncio for I/O-bound operations
  • Prefer concurrent.futures for CPU-bound tasks
  • Understand GIL limitations

docs.title: 19. Error Handling
docs.text:
• Create custom exception classes for domain-specific errors
  • Use context managers (with statement) for resource management
  • Include error recovery mechanisms where possible

docs.title: 20. Code Reviews
docs.text:
• Keep PRs focused and small
  • Include type hints in all new code
  • Document any non-obvious decisions
  • Update documentation when changing behavior

docs.title: 🛠 Development Practices
docs.text: This directory contains documentation about our software development methodologies, workflows, and best practices at DexaMinds. These guidelines help us maintain a consistent and efficient development process.

docs.title: 📚 Available Documents
docs.text:
1. Git Workflow Our branching strategy, commit message conventions, and PR guidelines.

  2. Testing Strategy Approaches to testing, including unit, integration, and end-to-end testing.

docs.title: 1. Version Control
docs.text:
• Use feature branches for all changes
  • Write meaningful commit messages
  • Keep PRs small and focused
  • Review all code before merging

docs.title: 2. Testing
docs.text:
• Write tests for all new features
  • Maintain high test coverage
  • Test in environments that match production
  • Automate testing where possible

docs.title: 3. Documentation
docs.text:
• Document public APIs
  • Keep READMEs up to date
  • Document architectural decisions
  • Include setup instructions

docs.title: 🔄 Development Workflow
docs.text:
1. Plan
  2. Create an issue/ticket
  3. Define acceptance criteria

  4. Break down large tasks

  5. Develop

  6. Create a feature branch
  7. Follow coding standards
  8. Write tests

  9. Document your changes

  10. Review

  11. Open a pull request
  12. Request reviews
  13. Address feedback

  14. Update documentation

  15. Deploy

  16. Run all tests
  17. Get approvals
  18. Merge to main
  19. Deploy to production

docs.title: 🛠 Tools We Use
docs.text:
• Version Control: Git, GitHub
  • CI/CD: GitHub Actions
  • Code Review: GitHub Pull Requests
  • Documentation: Markdown, MkDocs
  • Project Management: Jira, Linear

docs.title: 📝 Adding New Practices
docs.text: To propose a new development practice: 1. Create a new markdown file 2. Follow the existing format 3. Include examples and rationale 4. Submit a PR for review 5. Present to the team

docs.title: 🧠 DexaMinds Git & GitHub Training Guide (Using Git Bash)
✅ Install Git
docs.text: • Download from: https://git-scm.com/downloads

docs.title: ✅ Configure Git
docs.text:
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your.email@dexaminds.com"
git config --global core.editor "code --wait"  # Optional: Set VS Code as default editor

docs.title: ✅ Verify Configuration

docs.title: ✅ Clone a Repository

docs.title: 🔁 4. Commit Workflow
docs.text: This section outlines the standard Git commit workflow that every developer at DexaMinds should follow:

docs.title: 🧭 Step-by-Step Commit Workflow
docs.text:
1. Pull latest changes from the remote branch: bash git pull origin branch-name

  2. Check status of your working directory: bash git status

  3. Stage the changes you want to commit: bash git add file-name # Add specific file git add . # Add all changes

  4. Review staged changes (optional but recommended): bash git diff --cached

  5. Commit with a meaningful message: bash git commit -m "feat: add login validation for user form"

  6. Push your changes to the remote branch: bash git push origin branch-name

  7. Create a Pull Request on GitHub and request review.

docs.title: ✅ Create a PR
docs.text:
1. Push your branch to GitHub.
  2. Go to GitHub → Open your repo → Click "Compare & pull request".
  3. Add title, description, reviewers, and submit.

docs.title: ✅ Review a PR
docs.text: • Use GitHub UI to comment, approve, or request changes.

docs.title: ✅ Merge a PR
docs.text: • After approval, click "Merge pull request" on GitHub.

docs.title: 🧪 6. Best Practices
docs.text:
• Use meaningful branch names: feature/login-page, fix/api-error
  • Commit often with clear messages.
  • Always pull before pushing.
  • Delete merged branches to keep repo clean.
  • Use .gitignore to avoid committing unnecessary files.

Unstage file git reset file-name Commit git commit -m "message" Amend last commit git commit --amend View commit history git log View one-line log git log --oneline Create branch git checkout -b branch-name Switch branch git checkout branch-name Delete branch git branch -d branch-name Merge branch git merge branch-name Pull changes git pull origin branch-name Push changes git push origin branch-name View remote git remote -v Add remote git remote add origin <url> Remove remote git remote remove origin

docs.title: Testing Strategy
docs.text: At DexaMinds, we don’t just write code — we engineer confidence. Testing is not an afterthought or a checkbox; it is a core pillar of how we deliver scalable, secure, and resilient software. Our testing strategy is designed to support rapid iteration without compromising stability, allowing us to move fast and ship with pride.

docs.title: 🎯 Objectives
docs.text:
• Ensure functional correctness, security, and performance of our applications.
  • Enable fast feedback cycles through automation and continuous integration.
  • Promote a shared sense of ownership and accountability for product quality.
  • Encourage a test-first mindset to drive better architecture and design.

docs.title: 🧱 Testing Pyramid
docs.text:
We adopt a testing pyramid model that balances depth and speed, favoring a strong foundation of fast, reliable tests.

UI (End-to-End) Tests
 ────────────────────
Integration Tests
────────────────────
Unit Tests
────────────────────

docs.title: 1. Unit Tests (Foundation)
docs.text:
• Purpose: Test individual functions, components, or classes in isolation.
  • Tools: Jest, Mocha, JUnit, PyTest (language-specific)
  • Best Practices:
  • Cover edge cases and logic branches.
  • Keep tests small, focused, and independent.
  • Mock external dependencies (APIs, databases).

docs.title: 2. Integration Tests
docs.text:
• Purpose: Test interactions between modules (e.g., service layers and databases).
  • Tools: Supertest, Postman/Newman, SpringBootTest, Testcontainers
  • Best Practices:
  • Simulate real-world workflows.
  • Avoid excessive mocks to preserve integration realism.
  • Run regularly as part of CI.

docs.title: 3. End-to-End (E2E) Tests
docs.text:
• Purpose: Validate entire user flows and system behavior from the end-user perspective.
  • Tools: Cypress, Playwright, Selenium
  • Best Practices:
  • Test critical paths (e.g., login, checkout, data entry).
  • Avoid flakiness by managing test data and dependencies.
  • Run on staging environments post-deployment.

docs.title: 🔁 Test Lifecycle in CI/CD
docs.text:
Testing is seamlessly integrated into our CI/CD pipelines to ensure continuous quality:

Stage Test Type Trigger Pre-commit Linting, Unit Tests Developer local Pre-push / CI Unit + Integration Tests GitHub Actions / CI pipeline Pre-deploy E2E Tests Staging pipeline Post-deploy Smoke Tests, Monitoring Production

docs.title: 🛡️ Security Testing
docs.text:
• Automated tools: OWASP ZAP, Snyk, Dependency Scanners
  • Manual reviews for auth flows, role-based access, and input validation

docs.title: ⚡ Performance & Load Testing
docs.text:
• Tools: k6, JMeter, Locust
  • Run against staging before release cycles or after architectural changes

docs.title: 🧬 Contract Testing
docs.text:
• Tools: Pact, Dredd
  • Validates API agreements between microservices or frontend-backend interfaces

docs.title: 🧯 Chaos & Resilience Testing (optional but encouraged)
docs.text:
• Tools: Gremlin, ChaosMonkey
  • Ensure services degrade gracefully under stress or failure conditions

docs.title: ✨ Culture of Testing
docs.text:
Testing is not just a responsibility — it’s a mindset:

  • Write tests as you write code, not afterward.
  • Prioritize readability — future you and your team will thank you.
  • Fail fast, fix fast — treat failed tests as opportunities to strengthen the system.
  • Celebrate test coverage, but never sacrifice quality for numbers.

docs.title: ✅ Test Coverage Goals
docs.text:
Type Minimum Coverage Unit 80%+ Integration 60%+ E2E Critical flows only (measured by business impact)

Note: Quality > Quantity. 100% coverage with brittle or redundant tests can be more harmful than 80% meaningful coverage.

docs.title: 🧭 Conclusion
docs.text:
Our testing strategy ensures that every line of code we ship is reliable, maintainable, and ready for scale. In a fast-moving environment like DexaMinds, tests are not blockers — they are enablers. When we test well, we innovate with confidence.

Let’s continue to build boldly — with quality engineered at every step.

docs.title: Code of Conduct
docs.text: At DexaMinds, we believe that great products emerge not only from brilliant ideas and skillful engineering but from a culture rooted in integrity, respect, and collaboration. Our Code of Conduct sets the standard for how we work, communicate, and grow together — as individuals and as a collective striving toward innovation with impact.

docs.title: Our Commitment
docs.text:
We are committed to building an inclusive and inspiring workplace — one where creativity is celebrated, collaboration is second nature, and diversity of thought fuels our collective intelligence.

  • We prioritize respect, transparency, and empathy in all interactions.
  • We foster a culture where psychological safety and open dialogue enable everyone to speak up, take risks, and challenge the status quo.
  • We hold ourselves accountable for ensuring a safe, equitable, and empowering environment for all team members, partners, and contributors.

docs.title: Expected Standards of Behavior
docs.text: DexaMinds employees, contractors, and community participants are expected to embody these standards at all times:

docs.title: 🤝 Respect and Inclusion
docs.text:
• Embrace diverse perspectives, experiences, and cultural backgrounds.
  • Practice active listening and seek to understand before responding.
  • Avoid assumptions; engage with humility and curiosity.

docs.title: 🌱 Growth and Learning
docs.text:
• Provide and welcome constructive feedback generously and gracefully.
  • Celebrate progress — both individual and team — no matter how small.
  • Be open to evolving your views through dialogue, mentorship, and learning.

docs.title: 🔍 Integrity and Responsibility
docs.text:
• Do what’s right, not what’s easy — even when no one is watching.
  • Own your outcomes. Admit mistakes and treat them as growth moments.
  • Respect the confidentiality of sensitive information and decisions.

docs.title: 🧠 Collaboration and Innovation
docs.text:
• Be a multiplier: lift others up, share knowledge, and help ideas flourish.
  • Collaborate with purpose — align on goals, communicate clearly, and deliver with intent.
  • Take initiative, challenge assumptions, and experiment with courage.

docs.title: Unacceptable Behavior
docs.text:
The following behaviors are strictly prohibited and will not be tolerated:

  • Harassment, discrimination, or microaggressions based on race, gender, sexual orientation, disability, or any protected characteristic.
  • Bullying, intimidation, or intentional disruption of professional environments.
  • Spreading misinformation or engaging in deceitful practices.
  • Repeated disrespect of personal boundaries or professional expectations.

docs.title: Reporting Violations
docs.text:
If you witness or experience any behavior that contradicts this Code, we encourage you to speak up. You can report concerns to:

  • Your direct manager or mentor
  • The People Operations team at people@dexaminds.com
  • Or use our confidential reporting channel: ethics@dexaminds.com

Reports are treated with strict confidentiality. We are committed to fair investigation and protection from retaliation.

docs.title: Our Pledge to Evolve
docs.text:
Culture is a living system. As DexaMinds grows, so will our standards and practices. We actively revisit and refine our Code of Conduct to reflect our mission:

“To empower visionary minds and distributed teams to create lasting technological impact by harmonizing intelligence, empathy, and execution.”

Let this Code serve as both a compass and a commitment — to lead with purpose, create with passion, and build with people at the center.

docs.title: Communication
docs.text:
At DexaMinds, communication is a strategic advantage. It’s how we connect across boundaries, resolve complexity, and align toward impactful outcomes. In our hybrid and distributed environment, great communication is not accidental — it is intentional, inclusive, and infused with clarity and care.

Whether we are brainstorming on a whiteboard, collaborating asynchronously across time zones, or writing documentation, we communicate to build trust, reduce friction, and enable velocity.

docs.title: 🎯 Principles
docs.text: Our communication is shaped by the following guiding principles:

docs.title: 💡 Clarity Over Complexity
docs.text:
• Communicate simply and purposefully.
  • Eliminate ambiguity. Choose words that inform, not confuse.

docs.title: 🌍 Transparency is Default
docs.text:
• Share context openly unless confidentiality requires otherwise.
  • Document decisions and learnings for collective benefit.

docs.title: 🧠 Empathy in Every Exchange
docs.text:
• Seek to understand before responding.
  • Respect different styles, cultures, and bandwidths.

docs.title: 🕓 Asynchronous First
docs.text:
• Protect deep work time by reducing meetings where possible.
  • Use async updates, threads, and comments to keep progress visible.

docs.title: 🔁 Feedback is Fuel
docs.text:
• Share constructive feedback regularly and respectfully.
  • Create safe spaces for dialogue, disagreement, and growth.

docs.title: 🛠 Tools We Use
docs.text:
DexaMinds leverages a combination of synchronous and asynchronous tools — including Microsoft and modern collaboration platforms — to enable effective, flexible communication across teams and geographies.

Tool Purpose Microsoft Teams Real-time chat, group discussions, voice/video meetings, and team channels. Used for cross-functional collaboration and daily syncs. Microsoft 365 (Outlook, Word, Excel, OneNote, etc.) Formal emails, shared documents, collaborative planning sheets, and meeting notes. GitHub Code-centric discussions, pull request comments, issues, and changelogs.

⚡ Note: Use the right tool for the right message. Don’t escalate to meetings when a well-written message will do. Don’t bury critical decisions in chat threads — document them clearly.

docs.title: For Written Communication
docs.text:
• Use clear subject lines, structure with headers and bullets.
  • Set expectations: include action items, deadlines, and ownership.
  • Be inclusive: avoid inside jokes or region-specific slang.
  • Use emojis for tone, but sparingly in formal communications.

docs.title: For Meetings (Teams, GitHub, Office 365)
docs.text:
• Share an agenda in the invite or shared OneNote/Notion.
  • Designate a facilitator and a note-taker.
  • Capture and circulate key takeaways and next steps.
  • Keep meetings purposeful — start and end on time.

docs.title: For Emails (Outlook)
docs.text:
• Start with a brief summary or objective.
  • Use formatting (bold, italics, bullets) to improve scan-ability.
  • Cc relevant stakeholders, but avoid overloading threads.
  • Keep follow-up loops tight and actionable.

docs.title: 🌐 Global & Remote Communication
docs.text:
In a distributed environment, respect time zones and team schedules:

  • Use Microsoft Teams’ status indicators (e.g., “Do Not Disturb”) mindfully.
  • Favor async updates (e.g., Teams channels, email summaries).
  • Don’t expect immediate replies unless marked urgent and agreed upon.

We encourage using "core collaboration hours" for meetings and shared work, and respecting focus blocks to protect deep thinking.

docs.title: 💬 Communication Culture at DexaMinds
docs.text:
At DexaMinds, communication is not just about transmitting information — it's about building relationships, aligning purpose, and creating momentum.

We communicate:

  • To build context, not control.
  • To empower, not overwhelm.
  • To learn and lead, not just report.

“Every message, every meeting, every comment is an opportunity to build clarity, culture, and community.”

Let’s make each one count.

  8. Publishes events to message queue

  9. Response

  10. Service returns response
  11. API Gateway transforms if needed
  12. Response sent to client

docs.title: 🔄 Development Processes
docs.text: Welcome to the DexaMinds Development Processes documentation. This directory contains guidelines, workflows, and best practices that govern how we develop, review, and deploy software at DexaMinds.

docs.title: 📚 Documentation Index
docs.text:
1. Code Review Guidelines Standards and best practices for conducting effective code reviews.

  2. Deployment Process Step-by-step guide to our deployment pipeline and release procedures.

docs.title: 1. Branching Strategy
docs.text:
• main - Production-ready code
  • staging - Pre-production environment
  • feature/* - New features and improvements
  • bugfix/* - Bug fixes
  • hotfix/* - Critical production fixes

docs.title: 2. Pull Request Process
docs.text:
1. Create a feature/bugfix branch from main
  2. Make your changes with clear, atomic commits
  3. Open a PR with a descriptive title and description
  4. Request reviews from relevant team members
  5. Address all review comments
  6. Get required approvals
  7. Squash and merge

docs.title: 3. Versioning
docs.text: We follow Semantic Versioning: - MAJOR: Breaking changes - MINOR: Backwards-compatible features - PATCH: Backwards-compatible bug fixes

docs.title: 🛠 Tools We Use
docs.text:
• Version Control: GitHub
  • CI/CD: GitHub Actions
  • Code Review: GitHub Pull Requests
  • Documentation: Markdown, MkDocs
  • Project Management: Jira, Linear

docs.title: ✅ Quality Assurance
docs.text:
• Unit tests for all new features
  • Integration tests for critical paths
  • End-to-end tests for core user journeys
  • Performance testing for high-traffic endpoints
  • Security scanning in CI/CD pipeline

docs.title: 📝 Contributing
docs.text:
1. Check existing issues before creating new ones
  2. Follow the PR template
  3. Keep PRs small and focused
  4. Update documentation when making changes
  5. Add tests for new features

docs.title: 👨‍💻 Code Review Guidelines
docs.text: This document outlines the code review process and guidelines at DexaMinds to ensure high-quality, maintainable, and secure code.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
• Purpose
  • Review Process
  • Review Checklist
  • Best Practices
  • Common Issues
  • Review Etiquette

docs.title: 🎯 Purpose
docs.text:
• Maintain code quality and consistency
  • Share knowledge across the team
  • Catch bugs and security issues early
  • Ensure adherence to coding standards
  • Foster collaborative improvement

docs.title: 🔄 Review Process
docs.text:
1. Author
  2. Create a feature branch
  3. Make focused, atomic commits
  4. Write clear PR descriptions

  5. Tag appropriate reviewers

  6. Reviewer

  7. Review within 24 hours
  8. Provide constructive feedback
  9. Check for security concerns

  10. Verify tests and documentation

  11. After Approval

  12. Squash and merge
  13. Delete feature branch
  14. Update related documentation

docs.title: Code Quality
docs.text:
• [ ] Follows style guide
  • [ ] No commented-out code
  • [ ] No debugging statements
  • [ ] Proper error handling
  • [ ] Code is testable

docs.title: Functionality
docs.text:
• [ ] Meets requirements
  • [ ] Handles edge cases
  • [ ] No performance issues
  • [ ] Works as expected
  • [ ] No regression

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
• [ ] Input validation
  • [ ] No hardcoded secrets
  • [ ] Proper authentication
  • [ ] Data protection
  • [ ] Dependency updates

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
• [ ] Unit tests added
  • [ ] Integration tests
  • [ ] Test coverage ≥80%
  • [ ] Edge cases tested
  • [ ] Mock external services

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
• [ ] Code comments
  • [ ] API documentation
  • [ ] README updates
  • [ ] Changelog entry
  • [ ] Breaking changes

docs.title: Do
docs.text:
• Keep PRs small and focused
  • Write clear commit messages
  • Explain "why" in comments
  • Use meaningful names
  • Follow existing patterns

docs.title: Don't
docs.text:
• Mix refactoring with features
  • Commit commented code
  • Ignore linter warnings
  • Bypass security controls
  • Skip documentation

docs.title: 🔍 Common Issues
docs.text:
// Bad
const x = 1;

// Good
const maxRetryAttempts = 1;

docs.title: As an Author
docs.text:
• Be open to feedback
  • Explain your reasoning
  • Keep emotions in check
  • Thank reviewers
  • Learn and improve

docs.title: As a Reviewer
docs.text:
• Be respectful
  • Explain suggestions
  • Focus on code, not people
  • Acknowledge good work
  • Be timely in responses

docs.title: 🚀 Deployment Process
docs.text: This document outlines the standard deployment process for all services and applications at DexaMinds. Following these guidelines ensures consistent, reliable, and traceable deployments across all environments.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
• Environments
  • Deployment Workflow
  • Pre-Deployment Checklist
  • Deployment Steps
  • Rollback Procedure
  • Post-Deployment Verification
  • Emergency Procedures

docs.title: 🌍 Environments
docs.text: Environment Purpose Access Branch Development Local development Developers feature/* Staging Pre-production testing QA Team staging Production Live environment Ops Team main

docs.title: 🔄 Deployment Workflow
docs.text:
1. Development
  2. Code changes are committed to feature branches
  3. Automated tests run on each push

  4. Peer review required before merging to staging

  5. Staging

  6. Merge approved features to staging branch
  7. Automated deployment to staging environment
  8. QA and integration testing

  9. Performance and security validation

  10. Production

  11. Create release branch from staging
  12. Deploy to production during maintenance window
  13. Monitor and verify deployment

docs.title: ✅ Pre-Deployment Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] All tests are passing
  • [ ] Code review completed and approved
  • [ ] Documentation is updated
  • [ ] Dependencies are up-to-date
  • [ ] Database migrations are ready (if applicable)
  • [ ] Rollback plan is prepared

docs.title: Automated Deployment (Recommended)
docs.text:
# Deploy to staging
$ ./deploy.sh --env=staging --version=x.y.z

# Deploy to production
$ ./deploy.sh --env=production --version=x.y.z

docs.title: Manual Deployment
docs.text:
1. Build the application bash npm run build

  2. Deploy to target environment ```bash # Staging npm run deploy:staging

# Production npm run deploy:production ```

  1. Verify deployment bash curl -I https://api.staging.dexaminds.com/health

docs.title: 🔙 Rollback Procedure
docs.text:
1. Identify the previous stable version bash git log --oneline -n 5

  2. Rollback to previous version bash git checkout <commit-hash> ./deploy.sh --rollback --env=production

docs.title: 🔍 Post-Deployment Verification
docs.text:
• [ ] Verify application health
  • [ ] Check error logs
  • [ ] Monitor metrics and dashboards
  • [ ] Confirm critical user journeys
  • [ ] Notify stakeholders

docs.title: For Critical Issues
docs.text:
1. Immediate Rollback bash ./deploy.sh --rollback --env=production --emergency

  2. Contact

  3. Primary On-call: @ops-team
  4. Secondary: @engineering-managers

docs.title: 🐛 Debugging Common Issues
docs.text: This guide covers common issues you might encounter while working with the DexaMinds platform and how to resolve them.

docs.title: 🔍 General Debugging Approach
docs.text:
1. Check the logs ```bash # View application logs docker-compose logs -f

# View backend logs tail -f backend/logs/debug.log ```

  1. Verify services are running bash docker ps

  2. Check service status ```bash # Backend curl http://localhost:8000/health

# Frontend curl -I http://localhost:3000 ```

docs.title: 1. Database Connection Issues
docs.text:
Symptoms: - "Could not connect to database" errors - Timeout when accessing database

Solutions:

# Check if PostgreSQL is running
docker ps | grep postgres

# Check PostgreSQL logs
docker-compose logs postgres

# Try connecting manually
PGPASSWORD=postgres psql -h localhost -U postgres -d dexaminds

docs.title: 2. Frontend Not Connecting to Backend
docs.text:
Symptoms: - API requests failing with CORS errors - "Connection refused" errors

Solutions: 1. Verify backend is running 2. Check NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL in frontend .env 3. Ensure CORS is properly configured in backend

docs.title: 3. Dependency Issues
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Module not found errors - Version conflicts

Solutions:

# Frontend
rm -rf node_modules package-lock.json
npm install

# Backend
rm -rf venv
python -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate  # Windows: .\venv\Scripts\activate
pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: 4. Docker Container Issues
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Containers won't start - Port conflicts

Solutions:

# Stop and remove all containers
docker-compose down

# Rebuild containers
docker-compose build --no-cache

# Check for port conflicts
lsof -i :<port>  # Mac/Linux
netstat -ano | findstr :<port>  # Windows

docs.title: 5. Database Migration Problems
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Migration conflicts - Database schema out of sync

Solutions:

# Show migrations
python manage.py showmigrations

# Reset migrations (DEV ONLY)
find . -path "*/migrations/*.py" -not -name "__init__.py" -delete
find . -path "*/migrations/*.pyc" -delete
python manage.py makemigrations
python manage.py migrate --fake-initial

docs.title: Backend (Python/Django)
docs.text:
1. pdb Debugger python import pdb; pdb.set_trace() # Add to your code

  2. Django Debug Toolbar python # settings.py INSTALLED_APPS += ['debug_toolbar'] MIDDLEWARE += ['debug_toolbar.middleware.DebugToolbarMiddleware'] INTERNAL_IPS = ['127.0.0.1']

docs.title: Frontend (React/Next.js)
docs.text:
1. React Developer Tools
  2. Install Chrome/Firefox extension

  3. Inspect component hierarchy and props

  4. Browser DevTools

  5. Console for errors
  6. Network tab for API calls
  7. Application tab for storage

docs.title: "Module not found: Can't resolve 'module'"
docs.text:
# Delete node_modules and reinstall
rm -rf node_modules
npm install

docs.title: "Port already in use"
docs.text:
# Find and kill the process
lsof -i :3000  # Replace with your port
kill -9 <PID>

docs.title: "Database is locked"
docs.text:
# For SQLite
rm db.sqlite3
python manage.py migrate
python manage.py createsuperuser

docs.title: Slow Database Queries
docs.text:
# Add to settings.py to log slow queries
LOGGING = {
    'version': 1,
    'handlers': {
        'console': {
            'level': 'DEBUG',
            'class': 'logging.StreamHandler',
        },
    },
    'loggers': {
        'django.db.backends': {
            'level': 'DEBUG',
            'handlers': ['console'],
        }
    },
}

docs.text:
1. Use React DevTools Profiler
  2. Check bundle size with source-map-explorer
  3. Lazy load components

docs.title: 🖥 Local Development Environment Setup
docs.text: This guide will help you set up your local development environment for working with the DexaMinds platform.

docs.title: Hardware Requirements
docs.text:
• CPU: Quad-core 2.5 GHz or better
  • RAM: 16GB minimum (32GB recommended)
  • Storage: 50GB free space
  • OS: macOS 12+, Windows 10/11, or Linux

docs.title: Required Software
docs.text:
1. Git - Download
  2. Docker Desktop - Download
  3. Node.js (v18 LTS) - Download
  4. Python (3.9+) - Download
  5. VS Code - Download

docs.title: 1. Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone git@github.com:dexaminds/dexaminds.git
cd dexaminds

docs.title: Frontend
docs.text:
cd frontend
npm install

docs.title: Backend
docs.text:
cd ../backend
python -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate  # On Windows: .\venv\Scripts\activate
pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: 3. Configure Environment Variables
docs.text:
Create a .env file in the root directory:

# Database
POSTGRES_USER=postgres
POSTGRES_PASSWORD=postgres
POSTGRES_DB=dexaminds

# Backend
DEBUG=True
SECRET_KEY=your-secret-key
ALLOWED_HOSTS=localhost,127.0.0.1

# Frontend
NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL=http://localhost:8000

docs.title: 4. Start Services with Docker
docs.text: docker-compose up -d postgres redis

docs.title: 5. Run Database Migrations
docs.text:
cd backend
python manage.py migrate

docs.title: 6. Create Superuser (Admin)
docs.text: python manage.py createsuperuser

docs.title: Start Backend Server
docs.text:
cd backend
python manage.py runserver

docs.title: Start Frontend Development Server
docs.text:
cd frontend
npm run dev

docs.title: 🔍 Verify Your Setup
docs.text:
1. Frontend: Open http://localhost:3000
  2. Backend API: Open http://localhost:8000/api/
  3. Admin Panel: http://localhost:8000/admin/

docs.title: Backend Tests
docs.text:
cd backend
pytest

docs.title: Frontend Tests
docs.text:
cd frontend
npm test

docs.title: Port Conflicts
docs.text:
If you encounter port conflicts:

# Find processes using a port (Linux/Mac)
lsof -i :8000

# Kill the process
kill -9 <PID>

# On Windows
netstat -ano | findstr :8000
taskkill /PID <PID> /F

docs.title: Database Issues
docs.text:
If the database fails to start:

docker-compose down -v
docker-compose up -d postgres

docs.text:

  2. Update dependencies: ```bash # Frontend cd frontend npm install

# Backend cd ../backend pip install -r requirements.txt ```

  1. Run migrations if needed: bash python manage.py migrate
